sop.coo_evaluation:
# **Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member complaint & incident escalation** – no documented path for complaints (class issues, billing disputes, behaviour), near-misses, or safeguarding concerns; no clarity on who decides remediation
- **Financial close & reporting** – no SOP for monthly P&L review, cash reconciliation, or board reporting cadence
- **Coach performance management & termination** – no documented process for underperformance conversations, improvement plans, or offboarding
- **Member win-back/reactivation** – no systematic approach to churn prevention or win-back campaigns (distinct from marketing)
- **Duty of care incident response** – no protocol for injury on-site, medical emergency, or member welfare checks
- **Inventory & stock control** – no SOP for equipment maintenance logs, replacement ordering, or asset registers
- **Staffing & scheduling exceptions** – no process for shift swaps, sickness cover, or holiday approval chains
- **New class/product launch** – no gating process for piloting, feedback, or rollout of new offerings
- **Member data protection & GDPR** – no documented handling of data requests, retention, or breach response

---

# **Needs updating**

- **Client Onboarding (22 SOPs)** – likely missing: post-first-session follow-up timeline, how to move trial → paid member, recovery if payment fails on day 1
- **Coach Payroll & Expenses** – no link to timesheets or absence tracking; unclear if covers suspension/deactivation pay
- **Class cancellation protocol** – need clarity on notice period, member comms, and coach notification (especially last-minute illness)
- **Marketing (10 of 12)** – missing Email Newsletter & Blog Post, but also: how referral rewards work operationally, when to pause campaigns if capacity full
-

kpi.advice:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO ADVICE

## 🔴 IMMEDIATE DIAGNOSIS

**Biggest priority: June revenue collapsed 12.3% month-on-month (£20,198 → £17,710) despite 122 active clients.** Your AR/M (average revenue per member) fell from £165.56 to £120.48 — a 27% drop. This is your real problem, not headcount. The new studio (post-April relocation) should be driving *higher* revenue per member, not lower. Churn was 0% in May then reappeared (June data unclear but trend is spiking: 8.33% in April, 5.74% in May). Non-people expenses remain elevated (June £9,390 vs May £9,233), but April's anomaly skews the 3-month view — exclude it.

**Trend: Q2 (May–June, post-relocation) is underperforming Q1 (Jan–Mar) on profit margin and member value.**

---

## 📈 INCREASE REVENUE

- **Audit membership pricing now.** You're sitting at £120–170 ARPU. Benchmark your current tier structure: are you offering class packages, unlimited, drop-in rates? The new studio (2× size) should justify a 10–15% price rise on new sign-ups or renewal tiers. June's drop to £120.48 suggests members may be downgrading to lower-tier plans post-move. Test a "Premium Unlimited" tier at £45–50/month targeting the 147 members.

- **Investigate June revenue cliff.** Did you offer move-in discounts or pause billing? Did summer holidays hit attendance? Pull class attendance data for May vs June. If bookings dropped, implement a "Summer Challenge" upsell (£30 12-week program, bonus nutrition guide) to re-engage and fill quiet slots.

- **Plug the churn leak.** Attrition hit 5.74% in May and you lost members in June (from 122 down post-month). At £160 ARPU, each lost member costs £1,920/year. Implement 30-day win-back campaigns: email lapsed members with a 2-week free pass + 20% off 3-month renewal. Target cost: £50 per win-back; LTV payback in 3.75 months.

- **Unlock non-class revenue.** Your non-people costs are high (June £9,390 on £17,710 revenue = 53% of gross). You're likely

staff.advice:
• **JoJo is significantly overloaded at 133 sessions.** She's carrying 57% of the team's workload and is the sole or near-sole coach on high-demand classes (Build: Upper, Circuits, Mobility). Redistribute Build: Upper and Circuits to Eilis and Grace to reduce her load and build resilience into popular offerings.

• **Grace is under-utilised on non-Monday days.** At 52 sessions monthly, she has capacity beyond Mondays. Assign her one consistent weekly class on Wednesday or Thursday (e.g., Elevate or Build: Lower) to anchor timetable coverage and free JoJo further.

• **Eilis has spare capacity and should lead more classes.** She's at 48 sessions with room to grow. Expand her Elevate and Build: Full Body slots and introduce her to Circuits and Plate—lower-intensity classes suit her current skill level and reduce JoJo's load.

• **Personal Training and Body Composition Scans are too dependent on JoJo (44 + 20 = 64 sessions).** Cross-train Eilis formally on scans and grow her PT client base so she owns 30–40% of this revenue-critical work.

• **Strengthen Tuesday coverage.** JoJo owns Tuesdays alone; backfill her with Grace on a secondary Tuesday offering (e.g., Elevate or intro session) so demand doesn't bottleneck on one coach.

• **Introduce a monthly capacity review.** Track bookings by class and coach weekly to spot gaps before they force JoJo into crisis mode. Rotate class leadership quarterly to upskill Eilis and prevent burnout.

class_stats.suggestion:
**1. Consolidate low-attendance classes and reallocate slots to Build classes.** Engine, Capacity, Barbelle, Strongevity, and Games Night are consistently underperforming (12-62 attendees per class). Move these time slots to additional Build: Upper and Build: Lower sessions, which maintain strong demand week-to-week.

**2. Shift Monday and Thursday classes to higher-demand times or combine with Wednesday/Friday.** Monday (361) and Thursday (389) are your weakest days. Offer your most popular Build classes on these days at peak times (early morning or evening) to boost attendance, or consider reducing overall Monday/Thursday offerings.

**3. Add a second Engine or Capacity session with fresh positioning (time/instructor/marketing angle).** Before cutting these classes entirely, test one during a prime slot (Tuesday/Wednesday evening) with a rebrand or different instructor—low attendance might reflect timing rather than lack of interest.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Build for The Forge

**1. Audit what you're ACTUALLY delivering right now**
Before writing anything down, spend 2 hours shadowing coaches and asking: "What different things do you actually do with members?" (group classes, 1-on-1 coaching, form checks, nutrition consults, etc.). You likely offer more than you think—coaches are probably doing custom stuff off-book. Capture the real offerings first.

**2. Create two separate documents with different purposes**
- **Member-facing menu**: Marketing language, benefits-focused ("Build strength + confidence"), clear pricing, what's *included* at each tier (e.g., "Unlimited classes + 1 free form check per month"). Use this on your website and in sales conversations.
- **Coach guide**: The operational version—how long each service takes, who delivers it, decision rules (e.g., "nutrition consults only after 4 weeks of attendance"). This prevents coaches from over-delivering or under-delivering.

**3. Price anchoring—stack your offerings clearly**
Don't just list prices. Show tiers: *Membership £X/month* → *Add Personal Training at £Y/session* → *Add Nutrition Coaching at £Z/month*. This makes upsells obvious and makes members feel they're getting value at every level, not leaving money on the table.

**4. Get coaches to pressure-test it**
Share the draft with your top 2 coaches. Ask: "Would you recommend this to someone? Are we missing anything? Is anything listed here impossible to deliver consistently?" They'll flag reality gaps you'd miss alone.

**5. Lock it into your booking/billing system immediately**
Once finalised

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Refinement – Concrete Actions for Grace

• **Audit your actual drop-off points first.** Pull analytics from your current landing pages — where are visitors leaving? Which traffic sources have lowest conversion? This tells you which page to fix *first*, not all of them. Start with the worst performer.

• **Mine your existing customer conversations for language.** Review: emails to prospects, gym enquiries log, common questions at sign-up, Instagram DMs. Use *their exact words* not marketing jargon. If 5 people ask "is this intimidating for beginners?", that's your headline problem. Rewrite that section using their phrasing.

• **Create a visible FAQ section above the fold on each landing page.** List the 5-7 questions you hear most (beginners nervous? price objections? class times? segregated facilities concern?). Answer in 2 sentences max. Each answer removes friction *before* they call.

• **A/B test one change at a time.** Change the headline on your main landing page for 2 weeks, measure signups. Then change the CTA button. Don't overhaul everything — you won't know what worked. Move fast but measurably.

• **Clarify your positioning in the first 10 seconds.** The page should answer: "Why women-only?" "What makes The Forge different?" in the first visual/sentence. Many visitors won't scroll.

**Action today:** Pull last month's Google Analytics. Identify the worst-converting page. Schedule a 30-min call with 3 recent sign-ups asking "what almost stopped you from joining?"

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Executing Member Feedback System at The Forge

• **Start with post-class verbal captures, not forms.** Train 2-3 of your best staff to ask 1-2 specific questions after classes ("What'd make this better?" "Would you recommend us?"). Log answers in a shared spreadsheet daily. Forms feel corporate; conversation builds loyalty and surfaces real issues faster.

• **Make it tied to a visible win.** Display a monthly "Member Request Board" showing which feedback you've acted on (class time changed, equipment added, music adjusted). This proves you're listening and makes members feel invested in the gym's direction—not just surveyed and forgotten.

• **Route feedback directly to revenue decisions.** Use the data to inform class scheduling, instructor hiring, and retention campaigns. If 3+ members mention "childcare during 6pm classes," that's your next revenue problem to solve. If members rave about a particular instructor, use testimonials in your referral program immediately.

• **Automate the capture, not the response.** Use a simple Google Form QR code at each studio exit + manual notes in a shared doc. Spend 30 mins weekly reviewing, not building a system. The goal is *action*, not data collection theater.

• **Close the loop publicly.** When someone gives feedback, follow up: "We heard you wanted earlier morning classes—we've added a 6am slot." Tag them in social, ask them to come. One person's feedback becomes your next marketing angle.

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOP Execution for The Forge Services

**1. Start with your HIGHEST revenue/retention service first**
Don't write all five in parallel—you'll burn out. Identify which service drives the most member LTV or has the worst onboarding drop-off (probably personal training or Fuel Forward). Write that SOP first, test it with 3-5 members, refine it, *then* move to the next. Speed beats perfection.

**2. Structure each SOP around the member's first 7 days, not your process**
Don't write it as "Admin sends welcome email → PT does intake → member does orientation." Write it as: "Day 1 member receives X. Day 2 member does Y. Day 3 member experiences Z." This forces you to design the actual experience, not just document your workflow.

**3. Build in a "failure point" checklist for each SOP**
For each service, identify the top 3 reasons members quit or complain (ask your PTs and staff directly). Make those explicit in the SOP with a "if X happens, do Y immediately" section. Example for PT: "If member doesn't book session 2 within 48hrs of session 1 → PT sends personal message same day."

**4. Assign ONE person per SOP as the "owner"**
Grace writes personal training SOP with your lead PT. Staff member writes Fuel Forward with whoever runs that. Creates accountability and ensures the SOP actually works in practice (not just on paper).

**5. Put the SOP somewhere members see it—not just staff**
Upload each as a simple PDF guide or Notion page members access after signup. This cuts support questions by 40

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Content Calendar — Execution Blueprint

• **Start with a 90-minute audit this week**: Pull your top 5 performing posts from last 6 months (check analytics for engagement rate, not just likes). Identify the pattern — is it transformation stories, quick workout tips, member spotlights, or motivational quotes? Build your calendar around what *already* works at The Forge, not generic fitness content.

• **Batch-create monthly in one 3-hour session**: Pick one Saturday morning. Film 12-16 short clips of your best instructors mid-class, quick form tips, or member testimonials. Export variations (full length, 30-sec clip, Reels version). This kills the "I'll post something tomorrow" problem that kills consistency.

• **Use a dead-simple system**: Google Sheets with columns for [Date | Post Type | Caption | Image/Video Link | Platform]. Assign Tuesday = member spotlight, Thursday = class highlight/tip, Friday = motivational. Automate posting via Buffer or Later at 7am (when your audience is scrolling). No daily decisions = actually happens.

• **Tie posts to real gym moments**: Feature women hitting PRs, new class launches, or seasonal offers. Every post should either inspire sign-ups or deepen loyalty of existing members—don't waste the 3-4 slots on generic content.

• **Month 2, measure what moves revenue**: Track which post types drive trial class bookings or membership inquiries. Double down on *those* formats. Abandon the rest ruthlessly.

What's your current posting frequency looking like?

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Videography Sessions - Execution Plan for Grace

**Lock the schedule NOW (not later)**
- Book Jordan for the next 3 sessions back-to-back right now—8, 16, 24 weeks out. Put it in both calendars with payment locked in. This removes the "we'll sort it out" friction that kills consistency.

**Create a pre-shoot content brief (send 48hrs before)**
- Give Jordan 3-4 specific scenes you need: new member testimonial, quick workout clip, facility tour angle, transformation moment. Don't wing it. Members know they're coming = they show up looking sharp, you get usable footage, no wasted £.

**Assign ONE person (probably you) as content lead**
- You're responsible for corralling members that day, having a quiet space ready, and flagging any new angles you've noticed selling well. Jordan shoots; you direct. Otherwise it's chaos and you get generic gym footage.

**Repurpose immediately into a content calendar**
- 8-week cycle = split footage into social posts (2-3 per week for 4 weeks). Schedule these the week after shooting. Consistency in posting beats quality if posting dies between shoots.

**Use shoots as a sales tool**
- During shoot day, casually mention to members: "We're filming—love to get you in a quick testimonial." Members on camera stay longer and refer harder. It's not just content; it's member activation.

What's the main bottleneck you anticipate—getting members on camera, or staying disciplined with the 8-week cycle?

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Concrete Actions for Trial Enquiry Automations

• **Audit your current funnel first (today)** — Screenshot every step from "enquiry submitted" → "first session booked." Where do people drop off? Most trial systems leak at the follow-up stage. Fix that before automating broken processes.

• **Set up a 3-touch email sequence, not 5** — Day 0 (instant confirmation + gym tour video link), Day 2 (social proof: member testimonial), Day 5 (time-sensitive: "2 spots left this week"). Use Mailchimp or Klaviyo. Test subject lines with your actual audience (high open rates = relevance, not cuteness).

• **Automate the "show-up problem"** — SMS reminder 24hrs before their trial (not email—texts get 98% open rates). Offer a small incentive: "Bring a friend free" or "Free smoothie after session." This cuts no-shows by 40%+.

• **Measure ONE metric obsessively** — Trial-to-paid conversion rate (%), not just enquiry volume. If you're getting 20 enquiries but 2 conversions, automations won't fix a product problem. Track it weekly during the 6 weeks.

• **Hand-off point matters most** — Who contacts them after the trial? The same person who greeted them or a random staff member? Assign one "trial owner" per week. Personal follow-up (even a quick WhatsApp) converts 3x better than generic emails.

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Weekly Action Checklist Build for The Forge

**Start with role outputs, not tasks:**
- Map backwards from your revenue drivers: Which KPI does each person directly move? (e.g., JoJo = new member conversions, Eilis = class attendance/retention, Sarah = upsell/packages). Build their checklist *only* around those 2-3 metrics, not job duties.

**Use a one-page template per person:**
- Top section: their 1 primary KPI + target (e.g. "Convert 3 trials to paid members"). Middle: 4-5 specific actions that week that move it (e.g. "Follow up 5 trial members by Wednesday"). Bottom: what success looks like numerically by Friday. Laminate it or pin it in Slack—not a 10-page manual.

**Make it weekly-review ritual, not set-and-forget:**
- Every Monday morning (15 mins), you sit with each person, they fill in their checklist together. You catch misalignments immediately. They own accountability because they helped write it. This also flags capacity issues before they compound.

**Separate "core recurring" from "sprint adds":**
- Core checklist (same every week): their non-negotiable KPI actions. Below that: one flexible slot for seasonal campaigns, events, or ad-hoc projects. This prevents the list becoming bloated and ignored.

**Start with ONE person this week:**
- Pilot with JoJo first. Get it right, then clone/adapt for Eilis and Sarah. You'll spot what actually sticks vs. what sounds good on paper.

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Vision Session Execution for The Forge

**Before the meeting (do this TODAY):**
- Write out your SSO on ONE page (literally, one side of A4) — if you can't fit it, it's not clear enough. Include: why The Forge exists beyond "make money," what makes you different from Curves/Planet Fitness, and the one metric that matters most (member retention? revenue? referral rate?). This becomes your north star they navigate by.

**Structure the 90-day plan conversation (not a presentation):**
- Show 3-5 specific revenue/growth targets for Q next (e.g., "30 new signups in Feb," "4 partnership deals locked," "reduce churn to 8%") — not aspirational fluff. Then ask each team member: *"Which one owns a piece of this? What's YOUR number?"* This shifts them from listeners to stakeholders.

**Define lanes ruthlessly in the room:**
- Go person-by-person and say: *"Sarah, you own member experience & retention — if someone walks in angry, they talk to you first. What do you need from me and the team to win at this?"* Get their input. Ambiguity kills momentum faster than wrong direction.

**Lock commitments before they leave:**
- Have them write down (not you) their 90-day priorities and ONE weekly metric they're tracking. They own the visibility, not you chasing them for updates.

**Follow up with a 1-pager email within 24 hours** — this becomes your accountability document for the next 90 days.

What's your SSO right now, roughly?

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Audit & Rebalance for The Forge (30 Days)

**Week 1: Data Capture (Do This Now)**
- Pull last 4 weeks of attendance logs + staff schedules side-by-side. Identify whose classes are consistently full vs. sparse—this shows *actual* demand on each instructor, not assumptions. Also log who's covering admin tasks, cleaning, member queries outside their scheduled hours.
- Have Grace do a 15-min 1-on-1 with each team member asking: "What's taking 60% of your week?" You'll find hidden tasks (one person fielding all member complaints, another doing unofficial social media). Write it down verbatim.

**Week 2: Create the Rebalance Map**
- Build a simple spreadsheet: name, core role, current hours, peak stress tasks, strengths. Identify the 2-3 people actually overloaded (not busy-looking, but actually drowning). Don't move things randomly—find the *specific* task transfers that solve their problem AND match someone else's slack capacity.
- Example: If your lead instructor is doing all social media + class prep, shift social media to whoever has quieter afternoons (maybe your admin person expands into content).

**Week 3-4: Pilot & Lock In**
- Test new assignments for ONE week before full rollout. This catches friction fast (e.g., "I don't have the software access for this").
- Lock in writing: new role descriptions, weekly schedules, who does what admin task on which day. Grace signs off and posts visibly. Ambiguity kills rebalancing attempts.

**The Grace-Specific Move:** Schedule one follow

# Cutting 10% costs at The Forge — concrete moves

• **Audit your biggest 3 expense lines first** (likely: rent/lease, staff payroll, equipment/utilities). Don't waste time on minor subscriptions. Calculate the exact % each represents. A 10% overall cut probably means hitting one category hard (e.g., 15% staff efficiency gain through scheduling changes) rather than nibbling 2% everywhere.

• **Renegotiate supplier contracts NOW, not next renewal** — your equipment vendor, cleaning supplies, internet/IT. You've got leverage if you've been a steady customer. Threaten to switch. Get 3 competing quotes in writing first; use them as ammunition. This often yields 8-12% savings with minimal service impact.

• **Optimize class scheduling to cut instructor hours without cutting classes** — cluster off-peak sessions, use your most popular instructor twice daily instead of two staff for the same hours, trim evening classes that pull 4 people. Track real attendance data for the last 60 days. This is less painful than blanket layoffs and members barely notice.

• **Review your membership pricing model** — if you're discounting heavily or offering annual upfront deals, tighten those terms for new signups. Existing members stay; new cohorts pay more. This increases revenue simultaneously with cost cuts (double win).

• **Cut one thing you're maintaining but not promoting** — if you've got a steam room, pool class, or specialty service with <5 bookings/week, pause it. Redeploy that operational cost immediately.

**What's your biggest expense category right now?** That tells me where the real 10% lives.

# Monthly Finance KPI Update — Execution Playbook for Grace

• **Pull the raw numbers first thing (Monday morning).** Before any analysis: bank balance, member count, revenue vs. budget, churn rate, average revenue per member. 15 minutes max. This stops the "I'll do it later" trap that kills momentum.

• **Track only 5 KPIs, not 20.** For The Forge specifically: (1) Monthly Recurring Revenue (MRR), (2) Member churn %, (3) Cost per member acquisition, (4) Average class attendance rate, (5) Member lifetime value trend. Anything else dilutes focus.

• **Compare to last month AND same month last year.** One month tells you nothing. You need the seasonal pattern—summer slumps, January sign-up peaks. This tells you if August dip is normal or a problem.

• **Flag one actionable insight.** Don't just update a spreadsheet—write down *one thing* the numbers revealed (e.g., "Classes Tues/Thurs are 60% full; Mon/Wed are 45%—retarget ads to off-peak"). This is what drives your next decision.

• **Share top-line results with your team by Friday.** Keeps staff aligned on what matters. Transparency on revenue/churn builds accountability and shows how their work impacts the business.

What's your current update frequency—monthly, quarterly, or ad-hoc?

# Financial Forecasting for The Forge — Execution Plan

• **Day 1: Pull 24 months of actual data** — memberships by tier, churn rate, class attendance, PT revenue split, operating costs (rent, staff, utilities). You can't forecast garbage data. Get your accountant to export P&L, or do it manually from your booking system. This takes 2 hours max.

• **Build a 12-month revenue model in Excel** — three scenarios (conservative: 5% growth, realistic: 12%, optimistic: 20%). For each, map monthly membership projections against your capacity limits. If you're at 250 members, what does 300 look like? Where does churn kick in? This reveals your actual ceiling and guides hiring/space decisions.

• **Model your cash runway, not just profit** — many gyms look profitable but run out of cash. Calculate: month-to-month payroll, equipment replacement cycles, lease renewals. When does December (lower sign-ups) actually hurt your bank balance? This tells you if you need a credit line NOW or in Q2.

• **Use this to price your next offer** — once you know member lifetime value and cost-per-acquisition, you can calculate what you can spend on marketing without killing margins. If your average member generates £400/year profit, you can't spend £150 per sign-up.

• **Set three monthly KPIs** — active members, monthly churn %, average revenue per member. Review the first Friday of every month. Forecasting only works if you actually check reality against the model and adjust.

What revenue tiers does The Forge currently have? That'll tell me if there's a pricing opportunity hiding here.

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Profitability Mapping for The Forge

**Get the raw data today (1-2 hours max):**
- Pull last 3 months of revenue from your payment processor/gym management software, sorted by service type. If you use loose tracking, start NOW with this week forward.
- List fixed costs (rent, utilities, insurance) and split them proportionally by service (e.g., PT takes 30% of studio space → allocates 30% of rent).
- Add variable costs per service: instructor pay (% of class revenue is standard), equipment wear, supplies. PT instructor cost is often 40-50% of revenue; classes might be 25-35%.

**Build the one-page P&L (30 minutes in Excel):**
- Three columns: **Classes | PT | Add-ons** (protein shakes, merch, supplements).
- Row 1: Total revenue last 3 months. Row 2: Direct costs. Row 3: Allocated overhead. Row 4: Gross profit. Row 5: Margin %.
- Your target: Classes should hit 40%+ margin, PT 50%+, add-ons 60%+. If not, you've found your problem.

**Immediate action:**
- If PT is underpriced or underutilised, raise rates by £5-10/session THIS MONTH and test uptake. Better to lose one client than leave £500/month on the table.
- If add-ons are weak (usually 5-10% margin), stop stocking low-movers and double down on high-margin items (your own-brand protein is gold).

**Share the number with your team** (coaches/

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Audit Execution for The Forge — Concrete Actions

• **Map the actual journey first**: Have Grace (or her ops person) manually submit a test enquiry through EVERY channel she uses (website form, Instagram DM, Facebook, email direct) and track what happens. Document which automations fire, in what order, and spot the duplicates before touching anything in the backend. This reveals what's *really* happening vs. what she *thinks* is happening.

• **Fix the leak before optimizing**: The enquiry-to-trial flow likely breaks at the "get them in the door" step. Check: Are trial bookings actually being confirmed? Are reminder emails sending? Are no-shows tracked? Audit backwards from "did they show up?" — that's your real metric, not "did the automation send?"

• **Deactivate, don't delete**: Turn off suspect automations for 48 hours and monitor. If member experience doesn't change, they weren't working anyway. This is safer than deleting and discovering later you nuked something critical.

• **Assign ownership for ongoing accuracy**: Grace needs ONE person responsible for quarterly re-audits (not her). Automations drift — new tools get added, old ones forgotten. Quarterly = manageable rhythm.

• **Quick win**: She probably has 2-3 duplicate welcome sequences. Combine them into one, test it with next 5 enquiries, then roll out. This alone often improves conversion because messaging becomes consistent rather than muddied.

What's her current trial conversion rate? That's the north star for this audit.

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation for The Forge: Concrete Execution

**1. Pick the RIGHT 3 tasks first (10 mins)**
- **Email inquiries/onboarding responses** — likely 5-10 identical "Welcome, here's our class schedule/pricing/trial details" emails weekly. Use ChatGPT or Zapier to auto-draft responses, Grace reviews in 30 secs before sending.
- **Class cancellation/reschedule notifications** — automate the "Sorry, your 6am spin is cancelled, here's alternatives" message to affected members (saves 15 mins per cancellation).
- **Instagram caption + hashtag generation** — feed recent gym photos into an AI prompt that spits out 3 caption options. Grace picks one, posts in 2 mins instead of 15.

**2. Don't over-engineer — start with "dumb automation" (today)**
- Set up a **Zapier workflow** (free tier): when someone fills your contact form → auto-email them a templated response + add to CRM. Takes 20 mins to build, saves 3+ hours/month.
- Use **ChatGPT's custom instructions** to create a personal bot that knows The Forge's tone, then paste inquiries into it. Grace gets polished response drafts in 10 seconds.

**3. Measure the real payoff (first week)**
- Time each task before/after automation. Grace should reclaim **4-5 hours/month minimum** or the automation isn't worth maintaining.
- Track which automated emails get replies — if it's <70% response rate, the template is too generic.

**Go live with #1 and #2

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cybersecurity Execution for The Forge Website

• **Pick ONE quick win this week:** Install an SSL certificate (if not already done) + enable 2FA on your admin login. Takes 1-2 hours, costs £20-50/year, kills your biggest vulnerabilities immediately. Do this before anything else.

• **Audit your actual risk TODAY:** What customer data do you hold? Payment details? Membership records? The scope determines what matters. If you're not taking card payments directly (using Stripe/Square instead), you've already dodged 80% of compliance headaches.

• **Get a managed WordPress plugin (if that's your platform):** Wordfence or Sucuri handles backups, malware scanning, and firewall rules automatically—£10-15/month. Beats hiring a consultant at this stage. Set it to scan daily, forget it works.

• **Hand off the ongoing stuff immediately:** Cybersecurity isn't your competitive edge—fitness is. Delegate monthly updates and backups to your web host's managed service or a freelancer (Upwork, £20-40/month). Don't let this become your problem to maintain.

• **Customer trust angle:** Once locked down, add a small badge/mention on your privacy page. Women considering membership care about data safety. Frame it as a selling point in your next email to prospects.

**Reality check:** If your site is basic (contact form, membership info, no direct payments), you're 90% safe with the SSL + 2FA combo.
